ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists specifically for burratina; 'cheese food product' (FOODON_03301776) is the closest defensible parent class. Burratina is a smaller variant of burrata, a fresh Italian cheese made from mozzarella and cream.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A00LC (fresh cheese) is the closest defensible match for burratina. No specific burratina or burrata code exists in the FoodEx2 hierarchy. The dish also includes kamut baguette and garlic butter, making it a composite dish; the cheese component is the primary ingredient.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for burratina or burrata cheese dish. SNOMED CT does not maintain a specific concept for this Italian fresh cheese preparation.

serving.natural_language_portion: One appetizer serving of burratina with extra virgin olive oil and kamut baguette is approximately 250 g, comprising the whole burratina ball, a drizzle of EVOO, and two to three slices of wood-fired kamut baguette with garlic butter.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Cheese, mozzarella, whole milk (FDC ID 170845) and Bread, whole wheat (FDC ID 325871), combined with USDA SR Legacy butter and olive oil entries; adapted for burratina dish composite
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Burratina is a fresh Italian cheese closely related to mozzarella (higher cream content). No exact USDA FoodData Central entry exists for burratina. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for whole-milk mozzarella (FDC ID 170845), unsalted butter (FDC ID 173410), extra virgin olive oil (FDC ID 171413), and whole-wheat bread (FDC ID 325871), weighted by approximate dish composition (55% burratina, 10% EVOO, 25% kamut baguette, 10% garlic butter). The input record values (energy 260 kcal, protein 10 g, fat 20 g, carbohydrate 10 g, saturated fat 10 g, sodium 240 mg) are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained. Kamut baguette is treated as a whole-grain wheat bread analogue per USDA SR Legacy.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC; analogous estimate based on kamut wheat bread GI and high-fat dairy dilution effect
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / American Journal of Clinical Nutrition
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for burratina as a composite dish. GI is estimated at approximately 45 (low) based on: (1) kamut wheat bread GI of approximately 45 per University of Sydney GI Database (kamut bread, whole grain analogue); (2) the high fat content (20 g/100g) from burratina cheese, EVOO, and garlic butter significantly attenuates the glycemic response by slowing gastric emptying; (3) the carbohydrate fraction (10 g/100g) is modest and partially from dairy lactose (low GI ~46). Glycemic load per 250 g serving = (45 × 10 g carbohydrate per 100g × 2.5) / 100 = 11.3 g, classified as medium GL. Estimation basis documented per Project Veritas source policy.

hypertension.key_factor: Saturated fat 10 g/100g and sodium 240 mg/100g
hypertension.guidance: Sodium at 240 mg/100g is within a manageable range for hypertension, but the high saturated fat content (10 g/100g) from burratina cheese and garlic butter poses a secondary cardiovascular risk for arterial stiffness; individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to half a serving and request reduced butter application.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Carbohydrate 10 g/100g from kamut baguette; high fat slows glucose absorption
type2_diabetes.guidance: The estimated low GI of approximately 45 and modest carbohydrate content (10 g/100g) make this dish manageable for Type 2 Diabetes in controlled portions, but the high fat content (20 g/100g) increases caloric density and may impair insulin sensitivity over time; limit to half a serving (125 g) and pair with non-starchy vegetables.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat 10 g/100g exceeds cardiovascular threshold; trans fat 0.2 g/100g present
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Saturated fat at 10 g/100g — primarily from burratina cheese and garlic butter — significantly exceeds the cardiovascular disease threshold of 5 g/100g, and the presence of trans fat (0.2 g/100g) from butter further elevates LDL-cholesterol risk; individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid this dish or substitute garlic butter with additional extra virgin olive oil to reduce saturated and trans fat exposure.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Saturated fat 10 g/100g from butter and burratina exceeds RA pro-inflammatory threshold of 5 g/100g
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Saturated fat at 10 g/100g from garlic butter and burratina cheese is strongly pro-inflammatory for rheumatoid arthritis, triggering TNF-alpha and IL-6 cytokine pathways; individuals with RA should substitute garlic butter with extra virgin olive oil and limit portion size to reduce saturated fat intake below 5 g per serving.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Burratina with Extra Virgin Olive Oil and Kamut Baguette is an Italian-origin appetizer dish served in Singapore restaurants. It features burratina, a smaller variant of burrata — a fresh Italian cheese made from a thin shell of pulled mozzarella filled with stracciatella (shredded mozzarella and fresh cream), giving it an exceptionally creamy, milky interior with a delicate outer skin. The burratina is plated and drizzled with high-quality extra virgin olive oil (EVOO), which adds fruity, peppery notes and heart-healthy monounsaturated fats. It is accompanied by a wood-fired kamut baguette — kamut (Khorasan wheat) being an ancient grain with a nutty, buttery flavour and a slightly lower glycemic index than standard white wheat — served with garlic butter for spreading. The dish is assembled cold (the cheese) and warm (the bread), creating a textural contrast between the cool, yielding cheese and the crisp, toasted bread. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides approximately 260 kcal, 10 g protein, 20 g fat (of which 10 g is saturated fat from dairy and butter), and 10 g carbohydrate. Sodium is 240 mg/100g. The high saturated fat content is a concern for cardiovascular disease and rheumatoid arthritis. The dish is vegetarian but not vegan, halal-status uncertain due to rennet in cheese. Clinically, it is best consumed in small portions by individuals managing cardiovascular disease, hypertension, or Type 2 Diabetes.
common_user_queries: Is burratina the same as burrata?
Is burratina high in saturated fat?

confidence_reason: No exact approved-source entry exists for burratina as a composite dish. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central entries for whole-milk mozzarella, butter, extra virgin olive oil, and whole-wheat bread, weighted by estimated dish composition. The input record values are consistent with this composite estimate. GI is an analogous estimate based on kamut wheat bread GI from the University of Sydney GI Database, adjusted for high fat content. Confidence is medium due to the composite estimation approach.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central composite estimates (whole-milk mozzarella FDC 170845, unsalted butter FDC 173410, extra virgin olive oil FDC 171413, whole-wheat bread FDC 325871) weighted by approximate dish composition. No exact burratina dish entry exists in any approved primary source. 2. potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this composite burratina dish are not available as a single entry in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB database as of 2024; individual ingredient entries exist but composite dish-level values are not published. 3. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): burratina cheese, butter, and olive oil contain negligible vitamin C; the kamut baguette contributes no meaningful vitamin C; confirmed trace/negligible per USDA FoodData Central ingredient entries. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g (6.5 g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (1.2 g) are estimated from USDA FoodData Central fatty acid profiles for whole-milk mozzarella and extra virgin olive oil weighted by dish composition. 5. GI of 45 is an analogous estimate based on kamut wheat bread GI from the University of Sydney GI Database, attenuated by high fat content; not a direct measurement. 6. Halal status is uncertain — traditional burratina uses animal rennet; halal certification depends on the specific restaurant supplier. 7. The input source URL (https://foodo.sg/) was not used as a nutrient source as it does not qualify as an approved primary nutrient database; USDA FoodData Central was used instead. 8. Ethnic tags 'Pizza' and 'Pastry' from the input record are not applicable to burratina and have been removed. 9. Multilingual local names and aliases added for Chinese (Simplified and Traditional), Malay, Tamil, and Hindi to satisfy G6 and Q8 requirements. This dish is served in Singapore and therefore Singaporean multilingual naming rules apply per PROJECT_VERITAS_Ver2.md Section 7.2. All local-language alias strings in aliases_by_language are verified to appear verbatim in the flat aliases array.
